positive_prompt: Use case: stylized-concept. Asset type: original Nintendo 64 game environment production target, 4:3 composition. Image 1 is the existing Meridian Annex simulation-threshold layout reference; Image 2 is the skylight-spine design reference; Image 3 is the monitor-console prop reference; Image 4 is the four-pad simulation-dais reference. Create one highly polished, coherent environment concept for the Meridian Research Annex simulation-threshold corner. Preserve the functional architectural logic from Image 1—central illuminated portal, two readable simulation pads, wall cages and route depth—but redesign and dress it into a convincing authored late-1990s/early-2000s console RPG location. Integrate the skylight spine, one monitor console, and the four-pad dais as purposeful navigation and story landmarks rather than isolated objects. Use layered sun-bleached ceramic panels, oxidized dark-teal metal ribs, worn ochre flooring, analog machinery, cable conduits, maintenance access, cloth shade details, small tool trays, vent grilles, hazard edging, dust accumulation, and distant sector depth through the portal. Use premium hand-authored low-poly 3D environment language feasible for real Nintendo 64 hardware, strong silhouettes, chunky intentional geometry, sophisticated vertex-color gradients, and crisp 32x32/64x64-style materials, without photorealism or modern PBR gloss. Frame a native CRT-safe 4:3 gameplay-camera view at human eye height with clear foreground-to-background hierarchy, a traversable floor, the portal as focal point, readable simulation dais, no empty dead zones, and no extreme wide angle. Balance warm practical amber light with cool cyan skylight spill and cobalt shadows, using only one restrained violet Fracture signal. Maintain excellent readability at 320x240. Every prop must have a functional reason and the sector should remain plausible at 1,600–2,200 triangles plus instanced props.
negative_constraints: No empty greybox walls, primitive cubes as final forms, default materials, floating objects, unreadable fake interface text, signs with words, generic sci-fi corridor, cyberpunk neon overload, excessive detail impossible on N64, characters, creatures, logos, watermark, border, contact-sheet layout, recognizable commercial franchise design, or copied protected motifs.
